clean_story:
Scary stories. Folklore’s fingerprints.

The word folklore blends "folk" (ordinary people) and "lore" (old knowledge)—stories passed like a whispered game of telephone, changing just enough to stay alive. Horror borrows its scariest tools from these tales.

Folklore doesn’t just inspire monsters; it designs their rules. Vampires burn in sunlight because Slavic myths tied them to shadows—peasants feared the dead returning under cover of night. The jiangshi (Chinese "hopping corpse") wears Qing dynasty robes, its stiff limbs mirroring rigor mortis, a detail born from funeral customs. These aren’t random fright tactics. They’re survival guides disguised as stories: Don’t wander at dusk. Bury the dead properly. Trust no stranger.

Modern horror steals this precision. The Ring’s cursed videotape? A digital update of the yūrei’s vengeful spirit, which clings to objects in Japanese ghost lore. Hereditary’s demon king, Paimon, lifts his crown, horns, and horse-drawn chariot straight from 17th-century grimoires. Even jump scares follow folklore’s rhythm—like the wendigo’s howl in Algonquian tales, always heard just before the attack.

Three related subjects are urban legends today, how fairy tales warn, and why ghosts wear clothes.
